store: Preview Design System — Evidence & Citation Store

_note.purpose: Single source of evidence for the system, mirroring the tokens pattern: one store, referenced everywhere by id. manifest.json `rationale` entries cite these by `id`. docs/research.html is the human-readable render of this store (kept in sync; not auto-generated yet — see follow_up).
_note.hard_rule: NEVER invent a citation. The agent may DRAFT candidate citations but may never present an unverified one as fact, and never sets `verified` on its own authority. A fabricated source/finding/statistic shown to a client is a credibility-extinction event. Every verified citation must link to a real, checkable source.
_note.tiering_rule: Never promote a claim to a higher tier than its evidence supports. A convention is not an empirical finding; a judgment is not a standard.

verification_states.from research.html \u2014 pending fideli: Transcribed verbatim from docs/research.html WITH a checkable URL and not a pre-2020 dated source. Awaits a fast human fidelity check (confirm faithful transcription), after which the human sets verified_date.
verification_states.UNVERIFIED \u2014 pending human review: Needs full human verification. Applied to: paraphrased/extrapolated claims, any entry without a checkable URL, any pre-2020 dated source, and agent-drafted candidates not present in research.html.
verification_states.judgment \u2014 design call, no external: A tier=judgment entry: the system's own reasoned position. No external backing is claimed, so there is nothing external to verify; it is labeled honestly as judgment.

_note.verified_date_rule: Always null on creation. Only a human sets it, after review. The agent leaves it null.
_note.seeded_from: docs/research.html (v1.2) — every distinct referenced source/finding, mapped from that page's 2-tag (Evidence/Convention) system into the 4-tier taxonomy below.
_note.follow_up: Make docs/research.html render from this store so the two cannot drift. Not done this pass (research.html left untouched).

tiers.standard: Binding specifications — WCAG 2.2 success criteria, Section 508, ARIA APG, W3C CSS specs. Citable, not debatable, often legal weight. State as a requirement.
tiers.empirical: Findings from studies — Nielsen Norman Group, Baymard Institute, Pew, peer-reviewed HCI, eye-tracking. Citable, but with context: sample, domain, and recency matter.
tiers.convention: Established laws/heuristics and design-system consensus — Gestalt, Laws of UX, Material/Carbon/Primer, Bringhurst, citation-style guides, government usability guidance. Well-established practice, not measured fact.
tiers.judgment: Our reasoned call. Defensible by principle; no external backing claimed. Said plainly.

citations.finding: Non-text content has a text alternative; purely decorative media is marked aria-hidden so assistive tech skips it. Applied to bare wire-media placeholders in the v1.2 audit.
Information conveyed through presentation can be programmatically determined: semantic HTML, ordered headings, lists as lists, labels associated with inputs, asides programmatically distinguishable.
4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large. The grayscale ramp is designed against this: gray-50 (#767676) on white = 4.6:1; default text gray-95 = 19:1.
citations.notes: Contains specific contrast ratios (4.6:1, 19:1) tied to exact ramp values — confirm against tokens.css during fidelity check.

citations.finding: Color is never the sole means of conveying information or distinguishing an element. The grayscale system guarantees this by construction: status is carried by the text label, not hue, so badges, tags, and status-pills stay meaningful without color perception.

Content reflows to 320 CSS px wide with no horizontal scrolling. Mobile-first layouts, no fixed-pixel min-widths above 320px; in-page nav, mosaic gallery, and help-bar all collapse on narrow viewports.
3:1 for UI components and graphical elements. --color-border-strong (gray-50 = 4.5:1) is used for form input borders, secondary buttons, and bordered cards. Bumped from gray-40 in the v1.1 audit.
No loss of content when line height >=1.5 and paragraph spacing >=2x font size. Line height 1.5 default; no fixed text-container heights; overflow-wrap on flow text.
Content shown on hover/focus must be dismissable, hoverable, and persistent. Backs the hover-fill affordance and the CSS tooltip.

source.title: WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.13 Content on Hover or Focus

citations.notes: Source criterion is from WCAG 2.1 (2018, pre-2020) per research.html; flagged for full review although it is a stable normative criterion carried into 2.2.

citations.finding: All functionality available from the keyboard. Buttons/links native; tabs implement arrow + Home/End; accordion uses native details; mega menu and drawer respond to Esc.
Focus can move into and out of any component. The drawer's focus trap is the intentional dialog pattern: Esc and close-button release focus to the trigger.
Moving/auto-updating content can be paused, stopped, or hidden. The dismissable banner variant follows this.
Allow users to disable non-essential motion. @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) zeroes the --duration-* tokens at the root, killing transitions globally; skeleton shimmer respects it.
Focusable components receive focus in an order that preserves meaning. Source order matches visual order on every layout primitive; no tabindex > 0.
Link purpose is determinable from text or context. Linked-list, article-list, and breadcrumb avoid context-free 'click here' / 'read more'; icon-only buttons require aria-label.
Headings and labels describe topic or purpose. Every page leads with one descriptive h1; sub-sections use h2/h3 in order; form labels are required, not placeholder-only.
:focus-visible applies a 2px solid --color-focus ring with 2px offset on every interactive element. Inverse contexts override --color-focus to white. v1.1 fix.
A focused element is not entirely hidden by author content. scroll-margin-block-start on h1-h6 leaves clearance for sticky chrome on anchor jumps. v1.1 fix.
Focus indicator sizing/contrast guidance. The 2px offset prevents the ring being lost against component borders.
citations.notes: research.html labels this 'Evidence'. 2.4.13 is an AA criterion in WCAG 2.2; confirm the offset claim against the focus-ring tokens.

citations.finding: AAA 44x44 CSS px target size. The text-size widget and primary buttons meet this stricter floor (also Apple HIG).
Minimum target size 24x24 CSS px (AA, with exceptions); the system holds >=44px on primary controls and bumped breadcrumb/footer/in-page-nav links to 28px+ and tag--sm to 24px in audits.
The default human language is programmatically determined: <html lang="en"> on every page.
Errors are described in text. .wire-field__error renders next to the field with an associated aria-describedby.
Labels are provided when input is required. Every input requires a <label> via the .wire-field pattern; placeholders never substitute for labels.
UI components expose name, role, value programmatically. ARIA on tabs, mega menu, breadcrumb (aria-current=page), pagination, drawer (role=dialog, aria-modal), sidenav.
Status changes can be programmatically determined without moving focus. Loading buttons accept aria-busy; toast uses a polite live region; the text-size widget announces the new scale.
Tabs keyboard model: arrow keys move between tabs, Home/End jump to ends, Enter/Space activates; roving tabindex, aria-selected, aria-controls.

source.title: ARIA Authoring Practices Guide — Tabs Pattern

citations.finding: Tabs work best for parallel, peer content the user views one panel at a time; the selected tab must be clearly distinguished and tabs must look clickable. Keep the set small and avoid using tabs for sequential steps or for content users need to compare side by side.
citations.notes: Drafted for the Tabs migration. Confirm the exact article title/URL before use.

citations.finding: Jakob's Law: users spend most of their time on other sites and expect a component to work the way the same component works elsewhere. Tabs should follow the conventional tab interaction so they are understood without learning.
citations.notes: Drafted for the Tabs migration. URL is the canonical Laws of UX page — confirm before use.

citations.finding: Accordion disclosure pattern; single-open mode and +/- affordance. Built on native <details> for built-in semantics.

source.title: ARIA Authoring Practices Guide — Accordion Pattern

citations.notes: research.html also references MDN guidance on native <details> (no specific URL given).

citations.finding: Modal dialog pattern: role=dialog, aria-modal, focus trap, Esc to close, focus restored to trigger. Used for the mobile drawer and the modal.

source.title: ARIA Authoring Practices Guide — Dialog (Modal) Pattern

citations.finding: Tooltip pattern guidance; for icon-only buttons the tooltip is supplementary and aria-label is the accessible name.

source.title: ARIA Authoring Practices Guide — Tooltip Pattern

citations.finding: Table semantics: header scope, programmatic header/data association.

source.title: ARIA Authoring Practices Guide — Table Pattern

citations.finding: Navigation-menu trigger compromise: the mega-menu keeps an <a href> trigger for a no-JS fallback; the JS upgrade adds aria-haspopup + aria-expanded. Mirrors the W3C WAI-ARIA 'Menubar (Editor)' navigation example.

source.title: ARIA Authoring Practices Guide — Menubar example (navigation)

citations.notes: research.html paraphrases the W3C 'Menubar (Editor) example' without a specific URL; confirm the exact APG reference.

citations.finding: text-wrap: balance on headings; reduces orphan-line awkwardness in headlines.

source.title: CSS Text Module Level 4 — text-wrap: balance

citations.notes: The property is a W3C spec (standard); the 'measurably reduces' phrasing is a qualitative claim from the Chrome dev article — confirm wording.

citations.finding: Three-tier action hierarchy (primary, secondary, ghost) helps users identify the main action.
citations.notes: Pre-2020 source (2015).

citations.finding: Hover affordance via background fill (not underline-only); flat elements attract less attention and cause clickability uncertainty.

source.title: Flat UI Elements Attract Less Attention and Cause Uncertainty

citations.notes: Year not stated in research.html; confirm recency on fidelity pass.

citations.finding: Body text 16px (1rem) default, never below 14px in flow content, supports legibility/readability/comprehension.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency. research.html also cites WebAIM and iOS HIG default body for the same decision.

citations.finding: Max line length ~65-70 characters (max-inline-size: 70ch); comprehension optimal at 50-75 characters.

source.title: Line Length: How Many Characters Per Line? (Pernice)

citations.notes: research.html pairs this with Bringhurst's 45-75 character range.

citations.finding: No meaningful readability difference between serif and sans at 16px+; supports a system-sans default with serif reserved for editorial moments.

source.title: Serif vs. Sans Serif Fonts: Is One Easier to Read?

citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: Aside emphasis / visual hierarchy supports the callout pattern.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: Icon + title + description grids for landing pages; card as a content-grouping component.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: Left-rail orientation helps in deep IAs; backs the sidenav and doc-nav. Pairs with aria-current=page.
citations.verification: Re-fetched and read 2026-06-08; URL corrected to current canonical.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: Local navigation is contextual to the user's location: it shows the current node plus its siblings and applicable children, and should be visible but less salient than global nav. Placement is horizontal-below-global or left-vertical (the inverted-L); beyond ~4 tiers, breadcrumbs replace sibling display.

source.title: Local Navigation Is a Valuable Orientation and Wayfinding Aid

citations.verification: Fetched and read 2026-06-08; findings reflected on the Local navigation page.

citations.finding: Navigation must show where you are, not just where you can go. Indicate current location by highlighting the active item and, in deeper hierarchies, with breadcrumbs; location signals are often too subtle, so test by asking users where they are.
Reserve the right rail for secondary, contextual content (related links, an in-page table of contents). Banner/right-rail blindness means primary navigation placed there is widely ignored.
Keep side navigation shallow (about three levels); always keep exactly one link active; for deeper hierarchies, surface lower levels via links within parent pages rather than deepening the rail.

Side navigation suits one-to-three levels of hierarchy; nested lists express parent/child/grandchild; the current page is marked with a current class and aria-current; siblings render as same-level items.
Large clickable rows with descriptive secondary text (and visible counts/depth) outperform compact lists on category landings; aids wayfinding.

source.title: Category Pages Are Powerful Landing Pages

citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: Pull quotes aid scannability and emphasis.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency. research.html also cites Bringhurst on pull quotes as visual rhythm.

citations.finding: Vertical chronology with anchored date markers aids event-list scannability.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: When blocking (modal) is justified vs. nonmodal feedback.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: Hiding primary navigation behind a menu (hamburger / off-canvas) lowers discoverability and engagement; reserve it for where space genuinely forces it. Backs using the drawer for mobile nav, not as a default to hide working navigation.

source.title: Hamburger Menus and Hidden Navigation Hurt UX Metrics

citations.notes: Agent-drafted candidate; confirm the exact article, finding, and recency before use.

citations.finding: When to use transient vs. persistent feedback; backs the toast's polite live region.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: Site-level vs. transient messages; backs the banner.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: Empty states do three jobs: explain, reduce confusion, offer recovery.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency. Bordered variant references IBM Carbon Empty pattern.

citations.finding: Skeleton/progress indicators reduce perceived wait time. Pairs with aria-busy / aria-live; shimmer respects reduced-motion.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: When the IA fans out across audiences, a mega menu outperforms a deep hierarchy.

source.title: Mega Menus Work Well for Site Navigation

citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: On long-form pages an in-page TOC reduces scrolling and improves orientation; backs in-page anchor nav with scroll-spy.

source.title: Table of Contents: Design Best Practices

citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: Required-field marking with asterisk + a visible label note.
citations.notes: Year not stated; confirm recency.

citations.finding: Placeholder text never substitutes for a label.

source.title: Placeholders in Form Fields Are Harmful (Sherwin)

citations.notes: Pre-2020 source (2014).

citations.finding: Search input pattern with a leading magnifier icon — the most recognized search icon globally.

source.title: The Magnifying-Glass Icon in Search Designs (Hodge)

citations.finding: Labels above inputs (not beside); eye-tracking shows above-label completion is faster on mobile and matches saccade direction.

source.title: Form Field Usability: Avoid Multi-Column Layouts

citations.notes: Contains an eye-tracking claim; URL as given in research.html points at the inline-form-validation article. Give the claim+URL extra scrutiny on fidelity check.

citations.finding: Single-column form layout default; a 14% completion lift in tested checkouts.
citations.notes: Contains a specific statistic (14%); confirm the exact figure and its test context on fidelity check.

citations.finding: Inline error placement adjacent to the field; WCAG 3.3.1 fulfilled via proximity + aria-describedby.
35% of US adults have gone online specifically to figure out a medical condition; many escalate to a phone call. Backs the persistent phone / 'talk to someone' affordance.
citations.notes: Pre-2020 source (2013) AND a specific statistic (35%). Recency is weak; seek a current figure before using in the room.

citations.finding: Mobile-first hero scaling rationale. research.html states '60%+ of higher-ed inquiry traffic is mobile', attributed to the Web Almanac's Mobile Web chapter.
citations.notes: The '60%+ of higher-ed inquiry traffic' figure is an extrapolation: the Almanac reports general mobile-web data, not higher-ed-segmented traffic. Treat the specific number as unsupported until a segmented source is found.

citations.finding: Inline status-pill / badge convention.
Chip / removable-tag pattern.
Card pattern for grouping related content.
Step-indicator pattern for multi-step flows; ARIA via <ol> + aria-current=step.
Component-page conventions (title+eyebrow+lead+meta header; status pills; prev/next pager).
Tag / status-pill conventions; in grayscale, fill density carries differentiation and the text label is load-bearing.
Tile (card) pattern for grouping content.
Side-nav hierarchy patterns (local / secondary / section tree).

source.title: Carbon Design System — UI Shell Side Nav

citations.finding: Progress-indicator (stepper) pattern.
Bordered empty-state pattern.
Component-page header and status conventions.
Two-column docs layout (sticky nav + main) and prev/next foot navigation are the modern cross-system convention. Single-column collapse below 64em per WCAG 1.4.10.

source.title: Material Design, GitHub Primer, Radix, Shadcn, IBM Carbon, Gatsby Docs, Docusaurus

citations.notes: Consensus claim spanning several named systems (representative URL given); not a single source.

citations.finding: Line length (~66 characters / 45-75 range), leading, tabular alignment, dividers as silent punctuation, pull quotes and column hierarchy as visual rhythm.

source.title: The Elements of Typographic Style, 4th ed.

citations.notes: Book reference, no checkable URL, and pre-2020 edition. Authoritative but not web-checkable; cite by page if used with a client.

citations.finding: Numbered citation entries with stacked author / title / source metadata follow APA 7th edition and Chicago Manual of Style visual conventions for reference lists.

source.title: APA 7th edition; Chicago Manual of Style

citations.notes: No checkable URL transcribed from research.html.

citations.finding: Proximity: elements placed close together are perceived as a group. Underlies card/feature-grid grouping and grayscale hierarchy by spacing.
citations.notes: Agent-drafted candidate from the EVIDENCE-MODEL canon (§7); not currently cited in research.html. Needs a checkable source (e.g. Laws of UX / a Gestalt reference) before use.

citations.finding: Common region: elements within a shared boundary are perceived as grouped. Underlies card grouping via a shared bordered/subtle region.
citations.notes: Agent-drafted candidate from the EVIDENCE-MODEL canon (§7); not currently in research.html. Needs a checkable source before use.

citations.finding: Figure–ground: a dimmed backdrop pushes the page back and brings the dialog panel forward, focusing attention on the decision. Underlies the modal and drawer scrim.
citations.notes: Agent-drafted candidate from the EVIDENCE-MODEL canon (§7); not currently in research.html. Needs a checkable source before use.

citations.finding: Fitts's law: time to acquire a target is a function of its size and distance — larger, closer targets are faster and lower-error. Reinforces the >=44px primary-control target sizing.
citations.notes: Agent-drafted candidate from the EVIDENCE-MODEL canon (§7); not currently cited in research.html. URL is the canonical Laws of UX page — confirm before use.

citations.finding: Von Restorff (isolation) effect: an item that visually differs from its surroundings is more likely to be noticed and remembered. Backs the badge as a small, distinct status marker that draws the eye through contrast of form, not color.
citations.notes: Drafted for the Badge migration. URL is the canonical Laws of UX page — confirm before use.

citations.finding: Filtering and faceted navigation let users narrow large result sets; active filters should be shown as removable chips so users can see and undo what is applied.
citations.notes: Drafted for the Tag migration to ground the filter-chip use in NN/g research. Confirm the exact article title and URL before use.

citations.finding: Persistent, high-contrast help/contact affordances (§2.4) and user-controllable text sizing (§3.1) for older-adult users. Healthcare audiences skew older.
citations.notes: Vertical (healthcare) canon, already used since v1.1. Confirm the section numbers (§2.4, §3.1) against the current edition.

citations.finding: research.html attributes to the CDC Clear Communication Index: critical patient content (medication lists, instructions, directions) is regularly printed; language matching improves outcomes for limited-English-proficiency patients.
citations.notes: Inline claims are paraphrased and not row-linked in research.html (only the Sources list carries the CDC URL). Confirm the CCI actually makes these specific claims before citing.

citations.finding: Plain-language target: 6th-8th grade reading level on patient content. research.html states the average US adult reads at an 8th-grade level.

source.org: U.S. Dept. of Health & Human Services
source.title: Healthy People 2030 — Health Literacy

citations.notes: Contains a specific reading-level claim ('8th-grade average') paraphrased across HHS + plainlanguage.gov; verify the exact figure and its source.

citations.finding: Federal plain-language guidelines for clear, accessible content.

source.org: U.S. Federal Plain Language Action and Information Network
source.title: Plain Language Guidelines

citations.finding: Person-card pattern with a credentials line; patients use credentials as trust signals when selecting a provider.

source.org: U.S. Dept. of Health & Human Services
source.title: Hospital website transparency guidance (as referenced in research.html)

citations.notes: Vague attribution, no URL transcribed. Identify the specific HHS guidance or reframe as convention/judgment.

citations.finding: 4px spacing base. Industry consensus (Material, Carbon, Polaris); an 8px base would also be defensible.

source.title: Material / IBM Carbon / Shopify Polaris spacing systems

citations.notes: Named sources but no row-level URL; consensus claim. research.html lists this under 'What's convention, not evidence.'

citations.finding: Modular 1.25 type scale. Common practice (Material, Bootstrap, IBM Carbon); no published evidence of measurable readability advantage over 1.2 or a major third.

source.title: Material / Bootstrap / IBM Carbon type scales

citations.notes: Consensus claim, no URL. Honestly tagged convention (not evidence) in research.html.

citations.finding: Grayscale ramp granularity (12 steps). A designer call to provide enough hierarchy without overwhelming the file; Material uses 10, IBM Carbon 9.
citations.verification: judgment — design call, no external source claimed

citations.finding: Pill button radius. Aesthetic carry-over from the MotherDuck reference; no evidence pills outperform rounded rectangles for usability.
citations.verification: judgment — design call, no external source claimed

citations.finding: Specific motion durations (150/250/400ms). Material-guideline-aligned but no peer-reviewed support for these exact numbers.
citations.verification: judgment — design call, no external source claimed

citations.finding: System-font default over a custom face. Portability decision — every consumption surface (browser, Figma Make, artifacts) renders faithfully with no network dependency.
citations.verification: judgment — design call, no external source claimed

citations.finding: Working in grayscale forces hierarchy to come from weight, scale, proximity, and contrast — the same perceptual mechanisms Gestalt and contrast-sensitivity research describe. The system is thus a live demonstration of the principles it cites. (Defensible framing; the supporting Gestalt/contrast claims are themselves tiered convention/standard.)
citations.verification: judgment — design call, no external source claimed
citations.notes: EVIDENCE-MODEL §8 framing. Leans on gestalt-proximity and wcag-1.4.3 for its perceptual claims.

citations.finding: Canonical pattern for a custom select — a combobox trigger with a listbox popup and a defined keyboard model. A native <select> needs no ARIA.
Select should be a last resort in public-facing services; does not support <select multiple>; error messages begin with "Select".
Use a select for ~7–15 options with limited space; always provide a label; avoid auto-submission on change.
Option-count ladder: 2–5 → radio, 6–15 → select, 16–100 → combobox, 101+ → search with typeahead.
Native vs. custom trade-off: native works more easily with form submission and is easier on mobile; custom enables design-system styling.
A dropdown list selects a single item; prefer radios for a few options and order options logically.
Users aged 65 and older are 43% slower at using websites than users aged 21–55.
Long dropdowns cause overview, scrolling, and tab-flow problems; replace State/Country dropdowns with autocomplete.
Senior-friendly guidance: use pull-down menus sparingly; large buttons; single clicks.

source.title: Making Your Web Site Senior Friendly: A Checklist

citations.finding: Avatar guidance: circular avatars for people and rounded-square avatars for non-human entities (organisations, bots); always provide a fallback when no image is available.
citations.verification: UNVERIFIED — candidate from avatar research brief, pending human review

citations.finding: Avatar guidance: a fixed set of size steps and an image → initials → default-icon fallback chain; presence/status is layered as a separate indicator rather than baked into the avatar.
citations.verification: UNVERIFIED — candidate from avatar research brief, pending human review

citations.finding: Decorative images that add no information take an empty alt (alt="") so assistive technology skips them; informative images need a text alternative conveying their meaning.
citations.verification: UNVERIFIED — candidate from avatar research brief, pending human review

citations.finding: Photos of real, relevant people attract attention and can increase trust and engagement, whereas generic decorative stock imagery is frequently ignored.
citations.verification: UNVERIFIED — candidate from avatar research brief, pending human review

citations.finding: Operational transparency — making waiting and behind-the-scenes effort visible to people — can increase perceived value, trust, and satisfaction, even when the wait itself is unchanged.

source.title: Ryan W. Buell — research on operational transparency

citations.verification: UNVERIFIED — candidate from wait-time research brief; live-source check blocked by environment network policy, pending human review

citations.finding: Most UI icons are ambiguous without an accompanying text label; pairing an icon with a label (visible or accessible) measurably improves recognition and reduces user errors.
citations.verification: UNVERIFIED — candidate from icon-button research, pending human review

citations.finding: A toolbar groups a set of controls and manages focus with arrow keys (roving tabindex), exposing role=toolbar with an accessible name.
citations.verification: UNVERIFIED — candidate from button-group research, pending human review

citations.finding: Descriptive, front-loaded link text outperforms vague labels like 'click here' for scanning, comprehension, and screen-reader navigation.
citations.verification: UNVERIFIED — candidate from link research, pending human review

citations.finding: A disclosure is a button that controls visibility of a section of content, toggling aria-expanded; the native <details>/<summary> element implements this pattern.
citations.verification: UNVERIFIED — candidate from disclosure research, pending human review

citations.finding: Progressive disclosure — deferring secondary or advanced content until requested — reduces visible complexity and cognitive load while keeping detail reachable.
citations.verification: UNVERIFIED — candidate from disclosure research, pending human review

citations.finding: A labelled 'save for later' toggle plus a single recognisable portal entry door fit how patients compare care across visits and devices. Our reasoned position, no external study claimed.
citations.verification: judgment — design call, no external source claimed
